summary: Zach Lahn defeated Trump-backed Randy Feenstra in the Iowa Republican gubernatorial primary.
Lahn received 37.8% of the vote, while Feenstra received 37% with 98% of votes counted.
Iowa Gov. Kim Reynolds announced in April that she was not running for reelection.
title: Source Icon

bias: Left-Leaning coverage frames Pelley as a veteran journalist punished by power, stressing “murdering,” “collapse of values,” and even alleged efforts to push falsehoods or curry favor with the Trump administration; right-leaning coverage instead spotlights his “blowups,” “heated showdown,” and “insubordinate” or “crashout” behavior, portraying the firing as a justified cleanup.
The sharpest divide is whether the clash signals institutional capture and editorial interference or simply a toxic personnel dispute.
Both sides agree the episode exposes turmoil at CBS and centers Bari Weiss and Nick Bilton, but they read that turmoil through contrasting ideological lenses.
summary: On Tuesday, CBS News terminated veteran 60 Minutes correspondent Scott Pelley effective immediately, a day after he confronted the show's new executive producer, Nick Bilton, at a heated staff meeting.

Tensions at 60 Minutes escalated in recent months as employees clashed with CBS News Editor-in-Chief Bari Weiss over editorial direction under Paramount Skydance, the media company run by technology scion David Ellison.

During Monday's meeting, Pelley accused Weiss of "murdering" the newsmagazine, telling Bilton, "She was brought in to kill it," and questioned why Bilton accepted the role "knowing that you would never be welcomed here."
Executive producer Nick Bilton responded in a letter, stating, "Your antipathy to the future of the show has come through loud and clear," and informed Pelley his employment was terminated for cause.

Pelley's departure ends his nearly 40-year run at CBS News, deepening turmoil at 60 Minutes following recent exits of former executive producer Tanya Simon and correspondents Sharyn Alfonsi and Cecilia Vega.
title: CBS News fires ‘60 Minutes’ correspondent Scott Pelley after clash with new producer

bias: Left-Leaning coverage frames the Iowa race as a pivotal chance to “flip” a right-shifting state, stressing Democrats’ momentum while also spotlighting “dark money” and outside spending as evidence of internal pressure on progressives.

Right-Leaning coverage, by contrast, treats Hinson’s win as a GOP-strength moment, foregrounding “Trump-endorsed,” “secures,” and even dismissive labels like “lefty foe” to turn the matchup into a partisan showdown.
The biggest dividing lines are Trump’s endorsement, the open-seat stakes, and whether Turek is portrayed as a pragmatic “prairie populist” or a broader anti-establishment challenge.
Both sides agree the race is competitive and consequential, but they frame that competition through opposite ideological lenses.
summary: On Tuesday, Republican Ashley Hinson and Democrat Josh Turek won their respective primary nominations for U.S. Senate , setting up a November general election showdown for the open seat.
The race for this open seat began after incumbent U.S. Sen. Joni Ernst announced in September she would not seek a third term, leaving the position vacant for the first time since 2014.
Hinson, a U.S. representative endorsed by President Donald Trump, defeated former state Sen. Jim Carlin and pledged to be the president's "strongest ally" in the Senate if elected.
Overcoming state Sen. Zach Wahls, Turek emphasized plans to "protect and expand the Affordable Care Act" and reverse corporate influence in campaign financing.
Democrats view the contest as a critical opportunity to flip the seat, while Republicans consider defending the chamber pivotal to maintaining their Senate majority in November.
title: Iowa Senate race set between Democrat Josh Turek, Republican Ashley Hinson after projected primary wins

bias: Left-Leaning coverage frames the ruling as a partisan and racial setback, using loaded phrases like “GOP-friendly,” “dilutes Black vote,” and “majority-Black district” to spotlight harm to Black representation and cast the map as a Republican win; it also adds historical context, such as Jefferson Davis Day, to deepen the racial subtext.
The center leans more restrained, describing the map as “favoring Republicans” and noting the lower court’s finding of “intentionally discriminated,” while de-emphasizing emotional cues.
Both sides agree the map boosts Republicans and shrinks Black voting power, but they diverge over whether the story is chiefly legal procedure or a broader voting-rights crisis.
summary: On Tuesday, The Supreme Court granted an emergency request allowing Alabama to use a 2023 congressional map that eliminates one of two majority-Black districts, with the 6-3 conservative majority ruling the state is likely to prevail.

A three-judge panel previously ruled the map unconstitutional, finding intentional discrimination against Black voters in violation of the 14th Amendment despite earlier court findings regarding the Voting Rights Act.
Justice Sonia Sotomayor dissented, arguing the order disregards democratic values and the rule of law, while Democratic Rep. Shomari Figures would likely lose his House seat under the redrawn map.
Alabama officials pushed state primaries from May 19 to Aug. 11 to accommodate the map changes, enabling the state to restore its preferred map ahead of the upcoming elections.
title: Supreme Court lets Alabama use House map that favors GOP in midterms

bias: Left-Leaning coverage frames Rebecca Bennett’s primary win as a clean path against an “absent” GOP incumbent, stressing Kean’s prolonged absence and the district’s competitiveness; right-leaning pieces sharpen that into a more combative, suspicion-driven story with phrases like “vanished,” “mystery,” and “ripe for pickup,” while also foregrounding AP vote totals and House-control stakes.
The center is comparatively restrained, adding the key detail of a “personal medical issue” and treating the seat as “highly competitive” rather than scandalous.

Across both sides, the shared emphasis is Bennett’s Navy pilot background, the battleground district, and Kean’s absence, but ideological divides shape whether that absence reads as vulnerability, intrigue, or campaign context.
summary: Rebecca Bennett, a former Navy helicopter pilot and healthcare executive, won the Democratic primary in New Jersey's 7th Congressional District, NBC News projects, setting up a general election against Republican Rep. Tom Kean Jr.
Kean has not voted in the Capitol or been seen in public since March 5 due to a personal medical issue; his office stated Tuesday he will return 'within a matter of weeks.'
Bennett accused Kean of being 'nowhere to be found' on key district issues, while the Real Change PAC spent more than $600,000 attacking her, though the group's donors remain undisclosed.
President Donald Trump, who carried the district by 1 percentage point in 2024, endorsed Kean on Monday as 'working tirelessly.' The Cook Political Report rates the seat as a 'Toss-Up.'
Former New Jersey Gov. Tom Kean Sr. told CNN his son is under doctor's care and 'going to be fine.' Kean said he will transition to in-person work 'within a matter of weeks.
title: Bennett wins Democratic House primary to take on Kean in New Jersey

bias: Left-Leaning coverage frames Bass’s advance as a resilience story, even when it nods to a “shaky first term,” “wildfire,” and homelessness, while right-leaning coverage turns the race into a high-stakes horse race, spotlighting “JUST IN,” “gains ground,” and even “rig the election” suspicions.

The sharpest divide is over Bass’s meaning: left pieces stress she is “better than expected” and still a legitimate incumbent, whereas right pieces amplify Pratt as a “political outsider” and reality-TV challenger with momentum.
Both sides agree the runoff is triggered by no majority, but they split on whether that signals comeback or controversy.
summary: Incumbent Los Angeles Mayor Karen Bass advanced to a November runoff election after failing to secure the outright majority needed to win her second term during Tuesday's highly competitive nonpartisan primary.

Bass finished at the top of a crowded 14-candidate field with 36.5% of the early vote, navigating a particularly difficult re-election cycle fueled by growing voter frustration over a nearly $1 billion city budget gap and the slow rebuilding process following the devastating January 2025 Pacific Palisades wildfire.

The battle for the second runoff spot remains too close to call between insurgent conservative candidate and former reality TV personality Spencer Pratt, who currently holds second place with 30.1%, and progressive City Councilmember Nithya Raman, who follows closely in third with 20.2%.

Pratt’s right-leaning campaign saw a dramatic late-stage surge in public support, building massive national media attention and securing a high-profile endorsement from Donald Trump after utilizing aggressive campaign videos that tapped into voter anxiety regarding public safety, homelessness, and crime.

The upcoming November 3 general election will serve as a stark referendum on the city's political identity, forcing Bass to defend her progressive housing and labor coalition against either an aggressive challenger from the right or a former progressive ally running to her left.
title: Los Angeles Mayor Karen Bass advances to November runoff as she seeks second term

bias: Left-Leaning coverage foregrounds the child’s death and accountability, using sharper harm-centered language like “killed” and even “rammed,” while right-leaning framing is more compressed and event-led, stressing the “chase crash” and, in some wording, “causing the death” to assign responsibility without the same emotional weight.

The key dividing line is whether the story is portrayed as a tragic fatal pursuit or a manslaughter case with immediate punishment; center coverage sits between them, adding courtroom specifics like the conviction and remand.
Both sides agree on the prison sentence and fatal outcome, but their tone reflects broader tensions over police misconduct, punishment, and institutional blame.
summary: On Tuesday, former New York trooper Christopher Baldner was sentenced to 2½ to 7½ years in prison for the 2020 death of 11-year-old Monica Goods during a high-speed chase.
Baldner pulled over Tristin Goods for speeding on the New York State Thruway near Kingston just before midnight on December 22, 2020, prompting a high-speed pursuit.
Prosecutors alleged Baldner rammed the SUV twice on the highway, causing it to flip, while defense attorneys claimed the crash occurred after the vehicle cut off the trooper.
A jury convicted Baldner of manslaughter in March; he was previously acquitted of murder and reckless endangerment charges in the same case.
The 47-year-old Catskill resident was taken to jail in Ulster County immediately after sentencing, concluding a law enforcement career spanning nearly 20 years with the state police.
title: Ex-NY trooper sentenced to at least 2.5 years in prison in chase that killed 11-year-old girl

bias: Left-Leaning coverage frames the rematch as part of a bigger fight over who controls Congress, while right-leaning coverage leans into horse-race momentum, spotlighting Miller-Meeks’s primary win and the district as a “toss-up” or “most competitive” battleground.
The right’s language—“GOP’s,” “advances,” “rematch”—pushes a combative, partisan tone, whereas the left stays plainer and more institutional.
The main dividing lines are national stakes versus candidate-by-candidate traction, plus whether Bohannan is treated as a persistent challenger or a repeat runner.
Both sides agree the race is a significant November rematch in a swing district.
summary: On Tuesday, Democrat Christina Bohannan and Republican Rep. Mariannette Miller-Meeks secured their party nominations in Iowa's 1st Congressional District, setting the stage for a third consecutive rematch in November.
Bohannan defeated political newcomer Travis Terrell with 82% of the vote, while Miller-Meeks overcame marketing consultant David Pautsch by a far more comfortable margin than their 2024 matchup.
The nonpartisan Cook Political Report labels the race a 'toss up,' while Bohannan holds a slight financial edge with $4.3 million in cash on hand to Miller-Meeks' $4.2 million.
Miller-Meeks' 2024 victory by less than 800 votes makes Iowa's 1st Congressional District a top priority for the Democratic Congressional Campaign Committee, which aims to flip the seat.
Miller-Meeks frames the contest around 'Iowa values,' while Bohannan accuses the incumbent of being 'beholden to billionaires,' defining a campaign focused on economic costs and party control.
title: Republican Miller-Meeks, Democrat Bohannan head for rematch in Iowa congressional race

bias: Left-Leaning coverage frames Hamawy as a pro-Palestine doctor-activist, stressing Gaza volunteering and treating the win as a boost for progressive/Palestinian politics , while right-leaning outlets pivot to suspicion, foregrounding “Al Qaeda-linked,” “Squad-backed,” and “staunch critic of Israel” to cast the same victory as ideologically and security-charged.

The sharpest dividing line is whether his past charity ties are presented as solidarity or as a red flag; right coverage also leans on endorsements from Bernie Sanders and the “Squad” to signal factional identity, and on the crowded primary plus AP call to contextualize the result.
Both sides agree he won a deeply blue race, but they translate it through broader fights over Israel, activism, and Democratic identity.
summary: On Tuesday, progressive Democrat Dr. Adam Hamawy won the primary in New Jersey's 12th Congressional District, defeating 12 candidates to succeed retiring Rep. Bonnie Watson Coleman.
Hamawy's campaign gained momentum through endorsements from Sen. Bernie Sanders and Rep. Alexandria Ocasio-Cortez, alongside a $2 million advertising pledge from the super PAC American Priorities.

Media reports scrutinized Hamawy's 1995 testimony for Omar Abdel-Rahman, the "Blind Sheikh" convicted of terror plots; Hamawy dismissed the attacks as outdated "tropes" and said he was targeted because of his background.

Beyond the controversy, Hamawy leveraged his experience as a U.S. Army combat surgeon and medical relief work in Gaza, campaigning on Medicare for All, abolishing ICE, and opposing military aid to Israel.

Hamawy will face Republican Gregg Mele in the November general election; given the district's deep-blue lean, he is expected to win, potentially becoming the first Muslim to hold national office representing New Jersey.
title: Adam Hamawy, a Progressive Democrat, Wins House Primary in New Jersey

bias: Left-Leaning coverage frames the episode as an alarming Big Tech stumble, stressing how the bot was “tricked” or “just by asking” could expose passwords and account access; right-leaning coverage sharpens that into ridicule, using phrases like “easily fool,” “humiliating,” and “AI Fail” to portray Meta as repeatedly reckless, while also widening the lens to layoffs and “little-to-no human oversight.” The key dividing line is whether this is a narrow security flaw or proof that over-automation has backfired.
Both sides agree hackers exploited account-recovery loopholes and that Meta patched the issue, but they diverge sharply on whether the story signals corporate negligence, AI unreliability, or both.
summary: Meta has rushed to patch a critical security vulnerability in its AI support assistant after hackers discovered a surprisingly simple way to manipulate the chatbot into handing over control of premium Instagram accounts.

The exploit allowed attackers to hijack high-profile handles by simply asking the AI bot to change the account's registered email address, bypassing the need to hack the victim’s actual phone or email inbox.

Cybercriminals successfully used VPNs to mimic the geographic location of their targets, satisfying regional automated security checks before prompting the chatbot to send a verification code and password-reset link to an attacker-controlled inbox.

A wave of high-value and celebrity handles were compromised over the weekend, including the inactive Obama-era White House Instagram page, beauty retailer Sephora, and the personal account of US Space Force Chief Master Sergeant John Bentivegna.

The security lapse sparked immediate backlash from tech researchers, who criticized Meta for replacing human trust-and-safety teams with automated, easily fooled AI support bots following a series of corporate layoffs.
title: Hackers duped Meta AI support chatbot to steal celebrity Instagram accounts

bias: Left-Leaning coverage foregrounds the renewed search as a serious institutional response, stressing “new evidence” and even a “federal criminal investigation,” while right-leaning coverage strips the story down to a law-and-order recovery frame, using the sharper phrase “search for body” and de-emphasizing forensic doubts.
The center adds the most accusatory edge, with “possible murder” and “undercut” casting suspicion on Brian Hooker’s account.
A key dividing line is whether the case reads as a developing disappearance or a presumed fatal crime .
All sides agree on the Coast Guard’s Bahamas mission, but they diverge on whether the emphasis should be process, suspicion, or grim closure.
summary: On Tuesday, the Coast Guard arrived in Marsh Harbour, Bahamas , to search for missing American Lynette Hooker, a U.S. official told Fox News Digital.
Lynette disappeared on April 4, 2026, while boating near Elbow Cay in the Abaco Islands; her husband, Brian Hooker, claimed she fell from an 8-foot dinghy at night.
Investigators recently seized the sailboat Soulmate and are examining digital evidence after GPS data reportedly contradicted Brian's initial statements about their movements that evening.
Authorities are treating the case as a homicide, though former federal prosecutor Julie Rendelman cautioned that "no-body homicide cases" present significant evidentiary challenges.
Divers began scouring an area about 25 feet deep in the Sea of Abaco on Tuesday, while the tight-knit boating community expressed emotional shock at Hooker's disappearance.
title: Coast Guard divers arrive in Bahamas to begin new search for missing Michigan woman
Republican Rep. Ken Calvert advances in bid for Southern California House district that favors GOP

bias: Left-Leaning coverage frames the hiring as a scandal, leaning on alarmist cues like “stunned,” “alarm,” and “convicted Jan. 6 rioter” to stress the contradiction of placing someone tied to the Capitol attack in a “sensitive counterterrorism job.” Right-leaning coverage is de-emphasized here, but the center voice shifts to a more explanatory mode, asking “who is” the hire and noting his later remorse, which softens the portrait.

The key dividing line is whether the appointment is portrayed as institutional recklessness or a contextualized personnel decision; both agree the post was sensitive, yet they diverge over whether that fact signals danger or simply importance.
summary: On Tuesday, the Trump administration hired Elias Irizarry for a role in Special Operations and Low Intensity Conflict, despite his guilty plea for entering the Capitol on January 6, 2021.
Irizarry entered the Capitol Building through a broken window during the "Stop the Steal" rally, later pleading guilty to misdemeanor charges and serving 14 days in jail for disorderly conduct.
Four officials questioned the hiring of individuals linked to the January 6 attack for sensitive Pentagon roles, suggesting standard vetting processes may have been bypassed or overridden.
Joel Valdez, acting Pentagon press secretary, defended the appointment, calling Irizarry a "qualified, patriotic young professional" while criticizing the Washington Post for reporting on the hiring.

The appointment triggers scrutiny regarding vetting of political appointees with criminal records, as the Pentagon maintains its defensive posture amid external criticism of personnel decisions in sensitive security roles.
title: Pentagon Hires Elias Irizarry, Convicted in Jan. 6 Case, for Counterterrorism Role

bias: Left and center coverage frames the story as a public- health move—“re-engage” or “resume cooperation” with Gavi because of the Ebola outbreak—while the right leans into a policy-reversal narrative, stressing Trump’s role, prior funding cuts of about US$300 million, and the idea of “withholding money” to force reforms.
The right’s sharper rhetoric, with phrases like “anti-vaccine movement” and “changing tack,” adds a partisan and coercive tone absent from the more procedural left-center framing.
The main dividing line is whether this is an urgent health response or a politically charged reset over vaccine policy and thimerosal.
All sides still converge on Rubio, Gavi, and Ebola as the core facts.
summary: On Tuesday, U.S. Secretary of State Marco Rubio announced the U.S. will re-engage with the global vaccine alliance Gavi amid the Ebola outbreak in several African countries.
Health Secretary Robert F. Kennedy Jr. alleged last year that Gavi ignored safety, prompting the Trump administration to pull the group's funding entirely.
The U.S. had been withholding around $300 million in annual funding while Gavi moved toward newer vaccines without thimerosal, satisfying U.S. conditionality requirements.
Gavi Chief Executive Sania Nishtar stated she was "very encouraged" by the decision, as the U.S. made $50 million available for the Bundibugyo response in Democratic Republic of Congo and Uganda.
Health and Human Services and the State Department are engaging directly with Gavi to ensure "a constructive path forward" regarding transparency and accountability, Rubio said.
title: US to re-engage with Gavi vaccine alliance amid Ebola outbreak, Rubio says

bias: Left-Leaning coverage frames the meeting as a political show of support, emphasizing Schumer “stands by” Platner and the confident promise to “beat” Collins, while de-emphasizing the underlying allegations beyond a brief nod to “explicit texts.” Right-leaning coverage pivots sharply to scandal, using harsher cues like “Nazi-Tattoo Toting,” “embattled,” “sidesteps,” and “controversies” to cast Schumer as evasive and Platner as disqualifying.

The center stays more procedural, stressing that Schumer “shared few details” and was “peppered with questions.” All sides agree on the meeting, the refusals, and Collins as the strategic target, but they diverge over whether the story is about momentum or moral damage.
summary: On Tuesday, Senate Minority Leader Chuck Schumer met with Maine Senate candidate Graham Platner on Capitol Hill, repeatedly sidestepping questions about the candidate's recent sexting scandal while emphasizing the party's goal to defeat Senator Susan Collins.

Reports from The Wall Street Journal and New York Times revealed Platner sent sexually explicit messages to six women in 2024, compounding earlier controversies over offensive social media comments and a now-covered Nazi-linked tattoo.

Sen. Cory Booker said he has "concerns" about Platner, while Sen. John Fetterman compared the scandal to former California Democratic Rep. Eric Swalwell. Protesters chanted outside Democratic Senatorial Campaign Committee headquarters during the meeting.

Republican National Committee spokesperson Kristen Cianci called Platner "morally bankrupt" in a Tuesday statement, arguing that Democratic leaders' continued support shows how far they will go to advance their agenda.
Gov. Janet Mills remains on the ballot for the June 9 primary despite suspending her campaign; Maine law allows Democrats to replace Platner as the nominee before the July 13 deadline.
title: Schumer Meets With Platner as Democrats Weigh Maine Fallout

bias: Left-Leaning coverage emphasizes the policy’s breadth and trade consequences, spotlighting “most trading partners,” Canada, Mexico, and the EU, plus the tiered 10%/12.5% structure, while right-leaning coverage compresses the story into “Broad Tariffs ” and foregrounds the investigation as justification, with less country detail.
The center adds a domestic lens—“protect American workers” and “trade disparities”—that softens the partisan edge.
Rhetorically, the left leans on “forced labor trade practices,” the right on “broad” and “citing forced labor,” and the center stays more measured.
Both sides agree the tariff move is sweeping, but they diverge on whether it is mainly labor enforcement or economic hardball.
summary: The Trump administration has proposed imposing additional tariffs of 10% or more on dozens of major trading partners following a federal investigation into imports produced with forced labor.
The comprehensive probe conducted under Section 301 of the Trade Act of 1974 concluded that 60 investigated nations failed to properly enforce prohibitions against importing forced-labor goods.

The Office of the U.S. Trade Representative recommended a tiered penalty system, placing a 10% levy on nations like Canada, Mexico, Taiwan, and the United Kingdom, alongside a higher 12.5% penalty on China, Japan, India, South Korea, Brazil, and Switzerland.

U.S. Trade Representative Jamieson Greer defended the sweeping economic measures by declaring that the inaction of international trading partners creates an unlevel global playing field that unfairly burdens American commerce and workers.
The proposed duties will not take effect immediately, as they must first clear a mandatory regulatory period of public reviews and open panel hearings scheduled to begin on July 7.
title: US says it plans extra tariffs of 10% or more for most trading partners after forced labor probe

bias: Left-Leaning coverage frames Haaland’s win as a historic, identity-rich milestone, spotlighting phrases like “make history,” “first Native American woman,” and even “fight” against Trump-aligned policies, while right-leaning coverage pivots to a more combative horse-race narrative, using terms like “flip governor’s office,” “underdog,” and “pivotal moment” to stress Republican opportunity and New Mexico’s problems.
The left de-emphasizes raw vote mechanics and Hull’s platform; the right de-emphasizes Haaland’s symbolic significance.
Both sides agree on the result and the matchup, but they divide sharply over whether the story is mainly about representation or partisan power.
summary: Former U.S. Interior Secretary Deb Haaland secured a decisive victory in Tuesday's primary to become the Democratic nominee for governor of New Mexico, soundly defeating Bernalillo County District Attorney Sam Bregman with roughly 73% of the vote.

Former Rio Rancho Mayor Gregg Hull clinched the Republican gubernatorial nomination, securing about 46% of the vote to emerge victorious from a three-way race against small-business owner Doug Turner and cannabis executive Duke Rodriguez.

If elected in November, Haaland would make history as the first female Native American governor in United States history, adding to her trailblazing record as one of the first Native women in Congress and the first to serve in a presidential Cabinet.

The high-stakes race to succeed term-limited Democratic Governor Michelle Lujan Grisham will center heavily on managing the state's massive economy, which has reaped an unprecedented revenue windfall from soaring oil and natural gas prices amid the ongoing conflict in Iran.

The general election will present voters with a stark contrast on critical local issues, matching Haaland's progressive platform prioritizing universal child care, education funding, and tighter fossil fuel regulations against Hull's conservative platform focused on job creation, infrastructure improvement, and cracking down on violent crime.
title: Former Interior Secretary Deb Haaland and ex-Mayor Gregg Hull to face off for New Mexico governor

bias: Left-Leaning coverage leans into Paxton’s political risk, stressing that he could weaken his party’s chances, while right-leaning coverage sharpens the skepticism by portraying both hopefuls as “controversial” and in need to “shore up support.” The center and right also foreground Paxton’s Trump-linked itinerary with phrases like “campaign pilgrimage” and “White House huddle,” giving the story a more institutional, power-broker feel that the left de-emphasizes.

Across all perspectives, the shared core is the same: two Senate contenders crossing into Washington for party backing, but the split reveals whether the moment is framed as strategic consolidation, embattled vulnerability, or ideological theater.
summary: On Tuesday, Texas Attorney General Ken Paxton and Maine Democratic Senate candidate Graham Platner visited Washington to shore up party support, with Paxton scheduled to meet President Donald Trump.

Both candidates face internal concerns that their personal baggage could jeopardize winnable midterm races, as Paxton has weathered infidelity disclosures while Platner faces scrutiny over past text messages.

Senate Republicans previously described Paxton's behavior as "repulsive and disgusting," while Senate Minority Leader Chuck Schumer backed Maine Gov. Janet Mills before she suspended her campaign weeks ago.
Control of the Senate remains at stake for the final two years of President Donald Trump's second presidency, with Paxton facing Democratic nominee James Talarico in Texas.
Eight years ago, Texas Sen. Ted Cruz defeated Beto O Rourke by less than 3 percentage points, demonstrating how competitive Texas Senate races can be.
title: Paxton and Platner visit Washington to shore up support for their controversial Senate candidacies

bias: Left-Leaning coverage frames the story as a diplomacy-first effort to “help end” the Israel- Hezbollah conflict, stressing peacekeepers, ceasefire support, and even operational details like drones, radar, and satellite imagery; right-leaning coverage pivots to skepticism, using sharper language such as “failed,” “rebranding,” and “terrorist infrastructure” to portray UNIFIL as insufficient and the new mission as a response to border threats.
The key dividing line is whether the proposal is a constructive peacekeeping upgrade or a cosmetic fix.
Both sides agree on the UN presence, the Blue Line mission, and the 2,000-to-5,500-person range, but they diverge sharply on whether that presence is remedy or reproach.
summary: UN Secretary-General Antonio Guterres proposed three options for a new international force in South Lebanon to replace the United Nations Interim Force in Lebanon after its mandate expires on December 31, 2026.
Last year, the Security Council voted to terminate UNIFIL by year-end, ending a buffer presence that has operated along the 120-kilometer Blue Line between Lebanon and Israel since 1978.

Proposed personnel requirements for the three frameworks range from nearly 2,000 to more than 5,500 troops, aiming to provide operational monitoring, de-escalation support, and assistance to the Lebanese Armed Forces.
Rejecting the proposal, Israel's Ambassador to the UN Danny Danon told The Post that the UN is trying to "circumvent" the decision to end UNIFIL, calling it a "failed force."
Lebanon's ambassador to the UN Ahmad Arafa welcomed the report, while China's UN envoy Fu Cong urged the Security Council to ensure a continued presence to prevent a "security vacuum.
title: UN chief proposes options to help end Israel-Hezbollah conflict after peacekeepers leave Lebanon

bias: Left-Leaning coverage frames the standoff as a consumer harm story, using loaded terms like “blackout,” “go dark,” and “battle” to spotlight viewers losing access to the Stanley Cup Final and other local programming, while also stressing Scripps’s “record” or unusually high rates and even the timing around primary elections.
Center-Leaning reports stay more transactional, emphasizing the expired contract, 54 stations, and advance warnings.
Right-Leaning coverage narrows the lens to the inconvenience of an expired contract and event disruption, with less focus on rates or broader stakes.
All sides agree the retransmission impasse cut service across 36 markets.
summary: Millions of DIRECTV customers lost access to local stations on Monday after a retransmission consent dispute with E.W. Scripps Local Media, which blacked out 54 stations across 36 Nielsen designated market areas.
DIRECTV rejected what it characterized as the "highest rates we've ever seen" from Scripps, prompting the broadcaster to remove its stations after negotiations stalled over retransmission fees.
Fans of the Vegas Golden Knights can still watch the NHL Stanley Cup Final via the ESPN app using DIRECTV credentials, even in cities where Scripps owns the local ABC affiliate.
The American Television Alliance condemned the blackout Monday, urging Congress to update retransmission regulations; Scripps previously pulled 40 stations from Comcast Xfinity in April.

Blaming DIRECTV's private equity ownership, Scripps CEO Adam Symson claimed the provider ignores local community needs to protect "zombie channels" rather than prioritizing broadcast television and local journalism.
title: DirecTV-Scripps blackout hits 54 stations, leaving some without the Stanley Cup Final

bias: Left-Leaning coverage stays mostly restrained, simply repeating Flint’s nomination win, while center-leaning reporting adds election mechanics—“projected to win,” 52% of the first 39,000 votes, and broader district context—without the celebratory spin.

Right-Leaning coverage turns the same result into partisan affirmation, using phrases like “Trump-endorsed,” “fighting for Montana values,” and “take his fight to Washington” to cast Flint as a conservative standard-bearer.

The key divide is whether the win is a routine election outcome or proof of ideological momentum; both sides share the result, but differ sharply on what it signifies in the larger culture-war and Washington-power struggle.
summary: On Tuesday, The Associated Press projected Aaron Flint as the winner of the Republican primary for Montana's 1st Congressional District, securing 52% of the first 39,000 votes counted. Flint advances to the November 3, 2026, general election.

An Army veteran and host of Montana Talks, Flint campaigned on "consistent conservative" principles and Montana roots. He quickly secured endorsements from President Donald Trump, Representative Ryan Zinke, and Governor Greg Gianforte.

Flint decisively defeated Secretary of State Christi Jacobsen, who garnered 22% of the early vote, and orthopedic surgeon Al Olszewski with 19%. The primary victory followed Representative Ryan Zinke's surprise retirement announcement in March.

With the primary decided, Flint will face the Democratic nominee and Libertarian candidate Nick Sheedy in the November 3, 2026, general election. This three-way contest determines the district's next representative.

Montana voters narrowed fields across the state on Tuesday for several congressional seats, setting the stage for November's general election. The 1st District race remains a focal point for voters prioritizing economic issues and housing affordability.
title: Aaron Flint wins Republican nomination for U.S. House in Montana's 1st Congressional District

bias: Left-Leaning coverage casts Dr. Oz as evasive, with phrases like “ducks questions” and “hammering through” suggesting he rushed past scrutiny and sidestepped reporters, while right-leaning coverage turns the same briefing into a punchy victory lap, using words like “roasts,” “TDS-afflicted,” “Major,” and “TrumpRx” to celebrate policy wins and ridicule critics.

The sharpest divide is over the 160 added drugs: the right frames that as tangible progress and rising savings, while the left de-emphasizes the rollout in favor of accountability and communication gaps.
Both sides agree the briefing centered on drug prices, fraud, and health policy, reflecting a broader split between oversight and promotional, culture-war messaging.
summary: On Tuesday, Centers for Medicare and Medicaid Services administrator Dr. Mehmet Oz hosted the White House press briefing, filling in for press secretary Karoline Leavitt during her maternity leave.
Oz announced that 160 new medications are being added to the TrumpRx discounted-drug website, bringing the total number of drugs on the platform to more than 750.
The White House has invited high-profile officials to brief reporters while Leavitt is away; Oz followed Vice President JD Vance, Secretary of State Marco Rubio, and Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent.
Pressed on Bill Pulte's nomination as acting director of national intelligence, Oz stated, "I don't know anything more about Bill Pulte than you do," declining to elaborate.
Mehmet praised President Trump, who turns 80 this month, describing his mental acuity and energy as exceptional, stating, "That amount of energy does not exist in a vacuum.
title: 'Out of my lane.' Dr. Oz ducks questions during his turn in the White House briefing room

bias: Left-Leaning coverage frames the story as a child-welfare problem, stressing “treatment and support” and treating prison plans for “child gang killers” as troubling, even sympathetic, while right-leaning coverage turns the same policy into a law-and-order alarm, using harsher cues like “jail,” “special prison,” “drastic shift,” and “severe punitive measures” to spotlight punishment and a break from Sweden’s “social welfare and rehabilitation” tradition.

The key dividing line is whether the reforms are a humane response or a hard crackdown; both sides acknowledge gang violence and the 15-to-13 age change, but the left de-emphasized broader ideological context that the right amplifies, alongside concern about parliamentary stakes and minors as young as 11.
summary: Sweden plans to lower the age of criminal responsibility to 13 from 15, with Parliament expected to vote on June 15. Minors convicted of serious crimes will be locked in special prisons instead of youth welfare institutions.

Criminal gangs increasingly recruit minors as young as 11 to commit shootings and bombings across Sweden. Authorities report that 52 children under 15 were suspected of murder or attempted murder last year, prompting the government to declare its softer approach has failed.

At Rosersberg prison , one of three facilities being renovated for violent young offenders, daily life will focus on schoolwork. Prison governor Gabriel Wassman said inmates will have no phones, with cells locked nightly at 8 p.m., adding "they will grow up here."

Justice Minister Gunnar Strommer argues prison will act as a deterrent, yet Vilma Ruth of the Center Party counters that a 13-year-old is still a child. Denmark lowered its age to 14 in 2010, but researchers found the change had no effect on crime levels.

Prison governor Wassman expects the first young inmates to arrive after the summer. The government will review the measure after five years as Sweden combats criminal networks generating around 185 billion Swedish crowns annually.
title: Sweden Plans Special Prisons for 13-Year-Old Offenders

summary: Exiled journalists are increasingly choosing Canada as a hub to safely report on home-country abuses, with many establishing new bases in the Greater Toronto Area to escape persecution.

Andersson Boscan continues reporting on Ecuadorian corruption from Oakville, while Zahra Nader documents Afghan women's issues via Zan Times from Edmonton, both fleeing threats to hold power accountable.

Despite finding safety, these reporters face significant hurdles including online harassment and limited access to government documents, while advocates note they often lack necessary financial grants.

The Canadian Association of Journalists plans to launch an exiled journalists' caucus this summer to assist newsroom integration, while President Brent Jolly develops a directory connecting reporters with local outlets.

Growing hostility toward media in the United States makes Canada an appealing destination, while observers like Jesse Clarke emphasize the country should extend academic immigration efforts to include journalists.
title: In the News Today: Bouncy Castle Safety, Canada-U.S. Trade, Exiled Journalists

bias: Left-Leaning coverage foregrounds the family’s emotional strain, portraying Hunter Biden’s addiction as a “serious blow” and suggesting Jill Biden “suppressed” the topic, while right-leaning coverage strips away that context and reduces the story to a terse sign of regret, implicitly inviting skepticism about the Biden family.
Center-Leaning versions sit between them, emphasizing that openness can “offer hope” and adding interview context, including the memoir promo and the TV setting.

The main dividing line is whether the remark is framed as compassionate reflection or as a politically telling confession; both sides still converge on the basic apology and the addiction/recovery theme.
summary: On Tuesday, Jill Biden discussed her memoir 'View from the East Wing' on 'The View' with co-host Whoopi Goldberg, expressing regret that she did not speak more openly about her son Hunter Biden's past drug addiction.
Hunter's addiction spiraled after his brother Beau Biden died from brain cancer in 2015, a tragedy the family was 'partly in denial' about for years, Jill Biden acknowledged in her memoir.
Joe Biden's prostate cancer diagnosis put life into perspective for Jill Biden, helping her move past anger toward the Democratic Party regarding his 2024 reelection exit.
Reflecting on her time in the White House, Jill Biden told Goldberg that living in a 'fishbowl' was the hardest part of being first lady, citing constant scrutiny and loss of privacy.
title: Jill Biden says she’s sorry she didn’t talk more about son Hunter’s drug addiction

bias: Left-Leaning coverage frames the story as a narrow criminal allegation: scientists were “charged” with “lying” and bringing “deactivated mpox ” into the U.S., stressing the legal process and public-health context.

Right-Leaning coverage turns it into a broader alarm narrative, using loaded phrases like “JUST IN,” “conspiring,” “smuggling scandal,” and “bio-lab corruption” to suggest institutional rot, with “foreign nationals” and “endangering” heightening suspicion.
The key dividing line is whether this is an airport law-enforcement case or evidence of systemic lab failure.
Both sides agree on the charges, Detroit, and false statements, but differ sharply on scale, menace, and ideological meaning.
summary: Federal prosecutors charged two National Institutes of Health researchers on Tuesday with conspiracy to smuggle monkeypox into the United States. Vincent Munster, chief of the Virus Ecology Section at Rocky Mountain Laboratory, and Claude Kwe, a research fellow, face the charges.

Returning from the Republic of Congo on January 25, 2026, Munster and Kwe arrived at Detroit Metropolitan Airport with a large case. When questioned by Customs and Border Protection officers, they claimed it contained diagnostic equipment.

A subsequent investigation uncovered 113 vials stored in Styrofoam coolers. FBI testing of 20 samples revealed 17 contained deactivated monkeypox, one contained chickenpox, and two contained human DNA.

Both defendants face up to five years in prison on charges of smuggling and making false statements. Marcus Sykes of the Department of Health and Human Services called the incident a "breach of the public's trust."

Mpox remains a significant public health concern despite a fatality rate typically below 1 percent. An outbreak in Congo was linked to more than 2,000 deaths, underscoring the virus's potential severity.
title: Two researchers charged with allegedly trying to smuggle mpox through Detroit Metro Airport

bias: Left-Leaning coverage frames the story as another chapter in Santos’s misconduct, leaning on labels like “disgraced” and “new crimes” to connect the Kalshi probe to his earlier fraud history and Trump-linked politics , while right-leaning coverage stresses law-and-order suspicion with words like “probing,” “suspicious,” and “in hot water again,” often spotlighting his alleged bets and possible “new federal charges.” The key dividing line is whether the trade is portrayed as broader political corruption or straightforward wrongdoing.
Center coverage stays more restrained, emphasizing attribution and “under scrutiny.” All sides agree on the DOJ/Kalshi investigation and the Trump-address wager, but differ sharply on moral framing.
summary: The online prediction marketplace Kalshi has referred former New York Congressman George Santos to the Department of Justice and the Commodity Futures Trading Commission over suspicious trading activity.
Federal investigators are examining whether Santos engaged in insider trading by placing wagers that he would not attend President Donald Trump's State of the Union address in February.
Prior to the event, Santos publicly declared on social media that he would be sitting in the gallery for the speech, a move that sent the market odds of his attendance surging to roughly 75%.

Santos ultimately did not attend the address, later posting from an airport that his travel plans had fallen through, while allegedly profiting tens of thousands of dollars from the market's subsequent collapse.

Kalshi detected the irregular activity, froze the account tied to Santos, and reported the matter to federal authorities amid heightened legislative scrutiny over market manipulation on political betting platforms.
title: Former NY Rep. George Santos under investigation for alleged insider trading on Kalshi

bias: Left-Leaning coverage frames the exchange as a dangerous slide toward a “new war,” stressing stalled diplomacy, fragile ceasefire terms, and mutual escalation, while right-leaning outlets recast the same events as justified defense, foregrounding “self-defense,” “intercepted,” and even “blockade enforcement.” That split becomes sharpest around who initiated what: the left often treats strikes as part of a wider regional spiral, whereas the right portrays Iran as “aggressive” and U.S. actions as lawful retaliation.
Both sides note missiles, drones , and the Qeshm Island strike, but the left leans skeptical and cautionary; the right is more assertive and security-focused.
summary: On Tuesday, the United States and Iran exchanged fresh missile and drone strikes, jeopardizing efforts by Washington to secure a new ceasefire agreement with Tehran.
Hostilities flared after US Central Command disabled the Botswana-flagged M/T Lexie, an oil tanker that ignored repeated warnings over 24 hours in the Strait of Hormuz.
The Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps retaliated by targeting US assets in Bahrain and Kuwait, though the US military reported the attacks failed or were intercepted by regional defenses.
President Donald Trump disputed reports that communications had ceased, even as Iranian officials claimed a ceasefire in Lebanon is required for negotiations to continue.
The conflict remains mired in a stalemate as Iran faces record inflation and economic pressure, with the Strait of Hormuz effectively closed to maritime traffic amid the ongoing naval blockade.
title: Iran's IRGC attacks US Fifth Fleet headquarters, airbase, local media reports

bias: Left-Leaning coverage frames the event as a contained human crisis, stressing that one hostage was released and others were in “good health,” while right-leaning coverage turns it into a law-and-order spectacle, using sharper labels like “bank robber,” “cops swarm,” and “bombs strapped” to heighten danger and outrage.
The dividing line is the same core incident but different emphasis: left highlights de-escalation and civilian safety; right foregrounds criminality, “standoff,” and dramatic threat imagery.
Center coverage sits between them, focusing on police , lockdowns, and FBI response.
Across all sides, the shared focus is the bomb threat and hostage situation, but broader ideological priorities shape whether the story feels like rescue, crisis, or public menace.
summary: On Tuesday, the Bakersfield Police Department confirmed an active hostage situation at the Chase Bank on 17th Street and Chester in Bakersfield.
Officers responded around 1 p.m. after reports of a man allegedly holding at least one hostage with a bomb strapped to his body.
Mandatory evacuations are underway while police closed roads between 18th Street and Truxtun Avenue, as well as between Chester and H Streets.
Authorities locked down City Hall North, City Hall South, the Development Services Building, and Bakersfield Police Headquarters, restricting public access to these properties.
Officials advised the public to stay clear of the area as the Bakersfield incident remains under active investigation by law enforcement.
title: Man takes hostages at California bank as bomb threat prompts evacuations
Israel intercepts 'hostile aircraft' that crossed from Lebanon, military says
MSF Condemns Deadly Israeli Airstrike Near Hospital in Southern Lebanon

bias: Left-Leaning coverage frames Trump as cavalier and legally reckless, spotlighting “boring,” “really don’t care,” and “chirping” to mock his tone and fold in war-powers concerns; right-leaning coverage also seizes on “very boring” and “I really don’t care,” but treats them as a “dumbfounding backflip” while pivoting to Netanyahu, Lebanon, and the “simmering conflict” around Israel- Hezbollah .
The sharpest dividing lines are congressional approval, resurfaced-clip irony on the left, and alliance-management on the right.
Both sides agree the talks are in jeopardy and tied to regional escalation, revealing a shared focus on how Trump’s blunt style collides with broader Middle East tensions.
summary: On Monday, Iranian state media reported that Tehran suspended peace talks following Israeli strikes in Lebanon, prompting President Trump to tell CNBC, "I don't care if they're over, honestly."
Dismissing the stalled negotiations as "boring," Trump insisted he remains unconcerned about potential oil price impacts, adding, "Frankly, I thought they started to get very boring."

Reports emerged Monday that the president yelled at Israeli President Benjamin Netanyahu , calling him "f***ing crazy" during a call, though Trump later claimed on Truth Social they had a "very productive call."
Trump asserted that U.S. blockade efforts are "tougher" than Iranian actions, claiming Tehran loses $500 million daily, and predicted oil prices would "go down very quickly."

A deal to reopen the Strait of Hormuz should take shape over the next week, Trump said, while emphasizing his goal of preventing Iran from acquiring a nuclear weapon, noting the timeline "keeps shifting.
title: Trump Says He 'Couldn't Care Less' if Iran Talks Collapse, Calls Negotiations 'Very Boring'

bias: The left frames the story as pop-culture snark, using phrases like “full rom-com” and “reality villain” to make Maher’s praise feel ironic and playful, while the right turns the same exchange into a political-validation narrative, stressing a “huge endorsement,” Maher as a “leftist comedian,” and Pratt as a “Republican candidate.” That split deepens around the word “authentic,” which the left treats as a character note but the right repeats as proof of legitimacy, alongside warmer language like “cozy” and “warm and open.” The key dividing line is whether this is celebrity banter or an endorsement with education-policy implications; both agree Maher praised Pratt, but they disagree on what that praise means.
summary: Bill Maher praised reality TV star Spencer Pratt as an authentic candidate during a podcast.
Spencer Pratt criticized Karen Bass and Gavin Newsom for their handling of the wildfires that destroyed his home.
Pratt claimed union members support him despite union leadership backing Bass, highlighting a disconnect between leadership and members.
title: Bill Maher Backs Spencer Pratt For L.A. Mayor
Dr. Oz Says Trump Goes for Frequent Checkups because He 'Likes the Results'

bias: Left-Leaning coverage frames the $1.8 billion plan as a “slush fund” or “grift,” often tying it to “rioters” and Trump’s “allies,” and uses caustic terms like “kills,” “dumbest,” and “tanked” to stress corruption and outrage.

Right-Leaning coverage instead centers the “anti-weaponization” label, presenting the reversal as the administration “ditching” or “scrapping” a misguided policy, with “Republican backlash” signaling internal conservative pressure.

The key divide is whether the story is about patronage and abuse or about pushing back against government “weaponization.” Both sides agree Blanche said the fund would not move forward, but they diverge sharply on what that means.
summary: Acting Attorney General Todd Blanche announced Tuesday that the Justice Department is scrapping the nearly $1.8 billion "Anti-Weaponization Fund" intended to compensate allies of President Donald Trump following widespread political backlash and legal setbacks.

The Trump administration originally defended the fund as compensation for what officials insist was a "weaponized Justice Department" under President Joe Biden, though Democrats argued the proposal lacked necessary oversight and accountability.

A Virginia court temporarily blocked the fund, forcing the Justice Department to comply with a pause for at least two weeks as payouts would have been decided by a five-member commission appointed by Blanche.

During a tense private meeting last week, Republican senators voiced significant opposition, with more than half raising concerns that directly complicated legislative negotiations over funding immigration enforcement agencies.

Republicans returning to Washington on Monday stated they won't pass the Homeland Security spending bill until the White House works with Congress to place parameters on any compensation efforts, keeping the proposal a central point of contention.
title: Acting AG Blanche says Trump administration is nixing 'Anti-Weaponization Fund'
